questions.question:
Which of the following statements about the Ethics Committee in the Lok Sabha are correct?

  1. Initially it was an ad-hoc Committee.
  2. Only a Member of the Lok Sabha can make a complaint relating to unethical conduct of a member of the Lok Sabha.
  3. This Committee cannot take up any matter which is sub-judice.

Select the answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
The Ethics Committee of the Lok Sabha has specific procedural rules regarding its origin and functioning:

  • Initial Status: The Ethics Committee was indeed constituted as an ad-hoc committee in 2000 before becoming a permanent standing committee of the House in 2015.
  • Complaint Mechanism: Contrary to the second statement, any person can make a complaint against a member. If the complainant is not a member of the Lok Sabha, the complaint must be forwarded by a member of the Lok Sabha.
  • Sub-judice Matters: The Committee cannot take up any matter which is sub-judice (currently under judicial consideration).

questions.question:
Which of the following statements are correct in respect of a Money Bill in the Parliament?

  1. Article 109 mentions special procedure in respect of Money Bills.
  2. A Money Bill shall not be introduced in the Council of States.
  3. The Rajya Sabha can either approve the Bill or suggest changes but cannot reject it.
  4. Amendments to a Money Bill suggested by the Rajya Sabha have to be accepted by the Lok Sabha.

Select the answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
Money Bills are governed by specific constitutional provisions under Articles 109 and 110:

  • Article 109: This article explicitly outlines the special procedure for Money Bills, distinguishing them from ordinary bills.
  • Introduction: A Money Bill cannot be introduced in the Rajya Sabha (Council of States); it can only originate in the Lok Sabha.
  • Role of Rajya Sabha: The Rajya Sabha has limited powers. It can suggest amendments or approve the bill within 14 days, but it cannot reject or veto it.
  • Lok Sabha's Authority: The Lok Sabha is not bound to accept any amendments suggested by the Rajya Sabha. It can accept or reject all or any of the recommendations.

questions.question:
With reference to Union Budget, consider the following statements:

  1. The Union Finance Minister on behalf of the Prime Minister lays the Annual Financial Statement before both the Houses of Parliament.
  2. At the Union level, no demand for a grant can be made except on the recommendation of the President of India.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The process of laying the Union Budget is defined by Article 112 of the Constitution:

  • Laying the Statement: Under Article 112, it is the President of India who causes to be laid before both Houses of Parliament the 'Annual Financial Statement'. While the Finance Minister delivers the budget speech, the legal obligation rests with the President, not the Finance Minister on behalf of the Prime Minister.
  • Demand for Grants: According to Article 113, no demand for a grant shall be made except on the recommendation of the President.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding "Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam":

  1. Provisions will come into effect from the 18th Lok Sabha.
  2. This will be in force for 15 years after becoming an Act.
  3. There are provisions for the reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes Women within the quota reserved for the Scheduled Castes.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam (128th Constitution Amendment Bill)

This landmark legislation provides for women's reservation in legislative bodies:

  • Implementation: The reservation will come into effect after an exercise of delimitation is undertaken based on the relevant figures of the first census taken after the commencement of the Act. Therefore, it did not apply to the 18th Lok Sabha (2024 elections).
  • Duration: The reservation will remain in force for a period of 15 years from the date of commencement, though it can be extended by Parliament.
  • SC/ST Quota: The Act provides for the reservation of seats for women belonging to Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes within the total number of seats already reserved for SCs and STs.

questions.question:
With reference to the Parliament of India, consider the following statements:

  1. Prorogation of a House by the President of India does not require the advice of the Council of Ministers.
  2. Prorogation of a House is generally done after the House is adjourned sine die but there is no bar to the President of India prorogating the House which is in session.
  3. Dissolution of the Lok Sabha is done by the President of India who, save in exceptional circumstances, does so on the advice of the Council of Ministers.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The life of a session and the House is managed through various parliamentary instruments:

  • Prorogation: This is the act of terminating a session of the House. The President exercises this power under Article 85(2)(a), and like most executive powers, it is exercised on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers.
  • Timing of Prorogation: Usually, the President prorogues the House after it is adjourned sine die by the presiding officer. However, the President is not barred from prorogating the House while it is still in session.
  • Dissolution: Dissolution ends the life of the Lok Sabha. The President performs this on the advice of the Council of Ministers, except in cases where the government has lost its majority and no alternative government can be formed.

questions.question:
With reference to the Indian Parliament, consider the following statements:

  1. A bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses on its dissolution.
  2. A bill passed by the Lok Sabha and pending in the Rajya Sabha lapses on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.
  3. A bill in regard to which the President of India notified his/her intention to summon the Houses to a joint sitting lapses on the dissolution of the Lok Sabha.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The rules regarding the 'lapsing' of bills upon the dissolution of the Lok Sabha are as follows:

  • Pending in Lok Sabha: Any bill pending in the Lok Sabha lapses, whether it originated there or was sent to it by the Rajya Sabha.
  • Passed by Lok Sabha: A bill passed by the Lok Sabha but currently pending in the Rajya Sabha lapses.
  • Joint Sitting: If the President has notified the intention to summon a joint sitting before the dissolution of the Lok Sabha, the bill does not lapse.

questions.question:
With reference to the Speaker of the Lok Sabha, consider the following statements: While any resolution for the removal of the Speaker of the Lok Sabha is under consideration

  1. He/She shall not preside.
  2. He/She shall not have the right to speak.
  3. He/She shall not be entitled to vote on the resolution in the first instance.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
When a resolution for the removal of the Speaker is under consideration (Article 96):

  • Presiding: The Speaker cannot preside over the sitting of the House, though he/she may be present.
  • Right to Speak: The Speaker has the right to speak in, and otherwise take part in the proceedings of the House while the resolution is being considered.
  • Voting: The Speaker is entitled to vote only in the first instance on such a resolution, but not in the case of an equality of votes (casting vote).

questions.question:
Consider the following statements

Statement-I: In India, prisons are managed by State Governments with their own rules and regulations for the day-to-day administration of prisons.

Statement-II: In India, prisons are governed by the Prisons Act, 1894 which expressly kept the subject of prisons in the control of Provincial Governments.

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.option_1: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I
questions.option_2: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I
questions.option_3: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
questions.option_4: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct
questions.solution:
The legal framework for prisons in India is historical and constitutional:

  • State Management: Under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution, 'Prisons' is a State Subject (Entry 4). Thus, State Governments are responsible for the administration and rules of prisons.
  • Prisons Act, 1894: This colonial-era act continues to be the primary law governing prisons. It historically placed the subject under Provincial Governments, which translated to State Governments in the post-independence era. Statement-II correctly explains why Statement-I is the current reality.

questions.question:
With reference to Finance Bill and Money Bill in the Indian Parliament, consider the following statements:

  1. When the Lok Sabha transmits Finance Bill to the Rajya Sabha, it can amend or reject the Bill.
  2. When the Lok Sabha transmits Money Bill to the Rajya Sabha, it cannot amend or reject the Bill, it can only make recommendations.
  3. In the case of disagreement between the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha, there is no joint sitting for Money Bill, but a joint sitting becomes necessary for Finance Bill.

How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.solution:
Comparing Finance Bills and Money Bills:

  • Finance Bill (Type I) vs Money Bill: A Finance Bill (I) is treated as a Money Bill for the purpose of introduction but as an ordinary bill regarding its passage. Therefore, the Rajya Sabha can amend or reject a Finance Bill (I), unlike a Money Bill.
  • Money Bill Restrictions: The Rajya Sabha cannot amend or reject a Money Bill; it can only suggest recommendations within 14 days.
  • Joint Sitting: There is no provision for a joint sitting for a Money Bill. However, if there is a deadlock over a Finance Bill, a joint sitting is permissible and may be summoned by the President.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. A bill amending the Constitution requires a prior recommendation of the President of India.
  2. When a Constitution Amendment Bill is presented to the President of India, it is obligatory for the President of India to give his/her assent.
  3. A Constitution Amendment Bill must be passed by both the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha by a special majority and there is no provision for joint sitting.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
Constitutional Amendment Bills (Article 368) have unique requirements:

  • Prior Recommendation: Unlike a Money Bill, a Constitution Amendment Bill does not require the prior recommendation of the President.
  • Presidential Assent: The 24th Amendment Act of 1971 made it obligatory for the President to give assent to a Constitution Amendment Bill.
  • Passing Majority: Such bills must be passed by both Houses by a special majority (majority of total membership and 2/3rd of members present and voting). There is no provision for a joint sitting in case of a deadlock.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Constitution of India classifies the ministers into four ranks viz. Cabinet Minister, Minister of State with Independent Charge, Minister of State and Deputy Minister.
  2. The total number of ministers in the Union Government, including the Prime Minister, shall not exceed 15 percent of the total number of members in the Lok Sabha.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The organization of the Union Council of Ministers is guided by the following:

  • Ministerial Ranks: While the hierarchy (Cabinet, MoS, Deputy) exists in practice and under the Salaries and Allowances Act, the Constitution itself does not explicitly classify ministers into these four ranks.
  • Size Limit: The 91st Amendment Act, 2003, inserted Article 72(1A), stating that the total number of ministers, including the PM, shall not exceed 15% of the total strength of the Lok Sabha.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are the exclusive power(s) of Lok Sabha?

  1. To ratify the declaration of Emergency
  2. To pass a motion of no-confidence against the Council of Ministers
  3. To impeach the President of India

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
The Lok Sabha enjoys certain exclusive powers that the Rajya Sabha does not:

  • Emergency Ratification: This is not an exclusive power; both Houses must approve the proclamation of Emergency.
  • No-Confidence Motion: This is an exclusive power of the Lok Sabha. The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible only to the Lok Sabha (Article 75).
  • Impeachment: The power to impeach the President is shared by both Houses. Either House can initiate the charges, and the other House investigates.

questions.question:
With reference to Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha, consider the following statements:

  1. As per the Rules of Procedure and Conduct of Business in Lok Sabha, the election of Deputy Speaker shall be held on such date as the Speaker may fix.
  2. There is a mandatory provision that the election of a candidate as Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha shall be from either the principal opposition party or the ruling party.
  3. The Deputy Speaker has the same power as of the Speaker when presiding over the sitting of the House and no appeal lies against his rulings.
  4. The well-established parliamentary practice regarding the appointment of Deputy Speaker is that the motion is moved by the Speaker and duly seconded by the Prime Minister.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha

Provisions regarding the Deputy Speaker include:

  • Election Date: According to the Rules of Procedure, the Speaker fixes the date for the election of the Deputy Speaker.
  • Party Affiliation: There is no mandatory constitutional or legal provision regarding the party of the Deputy Speaker. However, by convention (not law), the post usually goes to the main opposition party.
  • Powers: When presiding, the Deputy Speaker has the same powers as the Speaker. Their rulings are final and cannot be appealed within the House.
  • Motion for Appointment: The motion is usually moved by the Prime Minister and seconded by another minister, or vice versa, but it is not a 'well-established practice' that the Speaker moves it.

questions.question:
We adopted parliamentary democracy based on the British model, but how does our model differ from that model?

  1. As regards legislation, the British Parliament is supreme or sovereign but in India, the power of the Parliament to legislate is limited.
  2. In India, matters related to the constitutionality of the Amendment of an Act of Parliament are referred to the Constitution Bench by the Supreme Court.

Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.solution:
Comparing the Indian and British Parliamentary systems:

  • Parliamentary Sovereignty: The British Parliament is a sovereign body (no judicial review of its acts). In India, the Parliament is not sovereign; its powers are limited by a written Constitution, federalism, and the power of Judicial Review.
  • Constitutional Bench: In India, Article 145(3) mandates that any case involving a substantial question of law as to the interpretation of the Constitution must be heard by a bench of at least five judges (Constitution Bench).

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha has equal power with Lok Sabha in
questions.option_1: The matter of creating new All-India services
questions.solution:
The Rajya Sabha and Lok Sabha share equal powers in several areas, but differ in others:

  • All-India Services: Under Article 312, the Rajya Sabha has the exclusive power to initiate the creation of new All-India Services.
  • Constitutional Amendment: Both Houses have equal power. A bill must be passed by both, and there is no provision for a joint sitting.
  • Removal of Government: The government is only responsible to the Lok Sabha; the Rajya Sabha cannot pass a No-Confidence Motion.
  • Cut Motions: These are related to the budget and are exclusive to the Lok Sabha.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. According to the Constitution of India, a person who is eligible to vote can be made a minister in a state for six months even if he/she is not a member of the legislature of that state.
  2. According to the Representation of People Act 1951, a person convicted of a criminal offense and sentenced to imprisonment for five years is permanently disqualified from contesting an election even after his release from prison.

Which of the above-given statements is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Legal and Constitutional eligibility for Ministers and Candidates:

  • Ministerial Eligibility: A person who is not a member of the legislature can be a minister for six months. However, the Constitution does not link this specifically to being 'eligible to vote'; they must become a member of the legislature within that period. Furthermore, the Supreme Court has ruled that a person who is disqualified from contesting (e.g., due to conviction) cannot be appointed as a minister.
  • Disqualification: Under the Representation of the People Act, 1951 (Section 8), a person convicted and sentenced to 2+ years is disqualified for the period of imprisonment and a further six years after release—not permanently.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The President of India can summon a session of parliament at such a place as he/she thinks fit.
  2. The Constitution of India provides for three sessions of the parliament in a year but it is not mandatory to conduct all three sessions.
  3. There is no minimum number of days that the parliament is required to meet in a year.

Which of the above-given statements is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The summoning and sessions of Parliament are governed by Article 85:

  • Place of Session: The President can summon Parliament to meet at any place he/she thinks fit.
  • Number of Sessions: The Constitution does not specify three sessions (Budget, Monsoon, Winter). It only states that six months shall not intervene between two sessions.
  • Minimum Days: There is no minimum number of days prescribed by the Constitution or law for Parliament to meet in a year.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. The Parliament (Prevention of Disqualification) Act 1959 exempts several posts from disqualification on the grounds of 'Office of Profit'.
  2. The above-mentioned Act was amended five times.
  3. The term 'Office of Profit' is well-defined in the Constitution of India.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The concept of 'Office of Profit' is intended to maintain the separation of powers:

  • Exemptions: The Parliament (Prevention of Disqualification) Act, 1959, lists several offices that do not disqualify the holder from being an MP.
  • Amendments: This Act has indeed been amended multiple times (including 1960, 1993, 1999, 2006, etc.).
  • Definition: The term 'Office of Profit' is not defined in the Constitution or the RPA 1951. Its meaning has been clarified through various court judgments.

questions.question:
With reference to the Legislative Assembly of a State in India, consider the following statements:

  1. The Governor makes a customary address to Members of the House at the commencement of the first session of the year.
  2. When a State Legislature does not have a rule on a particular matter, it follows the Lok Sabha rule on that matter.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Procedures in State Legislative Assemblies:

  • Governor's Address: Article 176 mandates the Governor to address the House at the commencement of the first session after an election and the first session of each year.
  • Rules of Procedure: Under Article 208, a State Legislature makes its own rules. In the absence of a specific rule, they often follow the Lok Sabha rules as a matter of practice or specific adoption.

questions.question: Regarding Money Bill, which of the following statements is not correct?
questions.option_1: A bill shall be deemed to be money bill if it contains only provisions related to imposition, abolition, remission, alteration or regulation of any tax.
questions.option_2: A Money Bill has provisions for the custody of the Consolidated Fund of India or the Contingency Fund of India.
questions.option_3: A Money Bill is concerned with appropriation of money out of the Contingency Fund of India
questions.option_4: A Money Bill deals with the regulation of borrowing of money or giving of any guarantee by the government of India.
questions.solution:
Article 110 of the Constitution defines a Money Bill. A bill is a Money Bill if it deals only with:

  • Imposition, abolition, remission, alteration, or regulation of any tax.
  • Regulation of borrowing of money by the Union Government.
  • Custody of the Consolidated Fund or the Contingency Fund of India.
  • Appropriation of money out of the Consolidated Fund of India (Note: Appropriation from the Contingency Fund is for unforeseen expenditure and handled differently).

questions.question: With reference to the Parliament of India, which of the following Parliamentary Committees scrutinizes and reports to the House whether the powers to make regulations, rules, sub-rules, by-laws, etc. conferred by the Constitution or delegated by the Parliament are being properly exercised by the Executive within the scope of such delegation?
questions.solution: The Committee on Subordinate Legislation is a parliamentary committee that examines whether the executive's power to delegate legislation (making rules, regulations, etc.) is being used correctly within the framework of the parent Act passed by Parliament.

questions.question:
With reference to the election of the President of India, consider the following statements:

  1. The value of the vote of each MLA varies from state to state.
  2. The value of the vote of MPs of the Lok Sabha is more than the value of the vote of MPs of the Rajya Sabha.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The President of India is elected by an electoral college consisting of elected members of both Houses of Parliament and Legislative Assemblies:

  • MLA Vote Value: The value depends on the population of the state (based on 1971 census) divided by the number of elected MLAs. Hence, it varies from state to state (e.g., UP has a higher value than Sikkim).
  • MP Vote Value: The value of the vote of every MP (both Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha) is the same. It is calculated by dividing the total value of all MLA votes by the total number of elected MPs.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

  1. Speaker of the legislative assembly shall vacate his/her office if he/she ceases to be a member of the assembly.
  2. Whenever the legislative assembly is dissolved the Speaker shall vacate his/her office immediately.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
The tenure and office of the Speaker of the Legislative Assembly:

  • Membership: The Speaker must vacate office if he/she ceases to be a member of the Assembly.
  • Dissolution: When the Assembly is dissolved, the Speaker does not vacate office immediately. He/She continues in office until immediately before the first meeting of the new Assembly after the general elections.

questions.question:
With reference to the Parliament of India, consider the following statements:

  1. A private member's bill is a bill presented by a Member of Parliament who is not elected but only nominated by the President of India.
  2. Recently, a private member's bill has been passed in the Parliament of India for the first time in its history.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution: A Private Member's Bill is any bill introduced by a Member of Parliament who is not a Minister. This includes both elected and nominated members who are not part of the government. While many have been introduced, only 14 Private Member's Bills have ever been passed in India's history, the last one being in 1970.

questions.question:
The Parliament of India exercises control over the functions of the Council of Ministers through

  1. Adjournment motion
  2. Question hour
  3. Supplementary questions

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
The Parliament ensures the executive's accountability through several mechanisms:

  • Question Hour: The first hour of every parliamentary sitting where MPs ask questions to ministers.
  • Supplementary Questions: Follow-up questions asked during Question Hour to seek further clarification.
  • Adjournment Motion: A motion to pause the normal business of the House to discuss a matter of urgent public importance, which involves an element of censure against the government.
